# Reviewable Architecture Studies {#sec-architecture-20-ontology}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"All models are wrong, but some are useful."*
>
> — George E. P. Box, *Robustness in the Strategy of Scientific Model Building* (1979)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** George E. P. Box, a British statistician who made major contributions to time-series analysis, famously noted that while all models are flawed, the good ones are practically useful. For architecture work, fast proxies and surrogate models are useful only when their scope, calibration, and failure modes are explicit; they can accelerate bounded exploration, but they do not by themselves justify commitment.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter formalizes how a bounded AI-assisted architecture study becomes reviewable. It distinguishes the study, its artifacts and architectural claim, the design loop used to execute and analyze the work, and the compact records that let another architect inspect the result.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What must a bounded AI-assisted architecture study make explicit before its
claims can be evaluated, challenged, and defended?
:::

Computer architecture has always depended on disciplined abstraction. An
architect rarely reasons directly from every transistor to every application
behavior. The field instead builds models, simulators, workload
characterizations, cost estimates, design rules, and review practices that make
large design spaces tractable. That quantitative tradition is central to
modern architecture practice [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach].
It also explains why Architecture 2.0 should not be framed as a sudden break
from the past. The continuity lies in disciplined abstraction, measurement,
tools, experiments, and judgment. The shift is that AI can now participate
across formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and
defense, including parts of the process that produces the architectural claim.
Wider participation changes the failure mode. A plausible error can propagate
through tools and decisions faster when scope, counterevidence, and authority
remain implicit.

What becomes first-class alongside the artifact is the bounded architecture
study that gives the artifact meaning. A study frames the question, identifies
alternatives, connects them to tools, runs experiments, interprets mechanisms,
and supports a finding or decision. The study must expose its execution path.
When that path iterates, it must show enough of the design loop, including state,
permitted action, feedback, evidence, revision, and decision authority, for
another architect to inspect how the work was carried out
[@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. The compact execution view introduced
in @sec-moonshot is one reading of those obligations, not a form the reader must
fill out. Without that structure, an AI system may still produce plausible
text, code, or configurations, but its output cannot support a consequential
architecture claim on plausibility alone.

Other fields do not supply a ready-made Architecture 2.0 standard. They do show
which properties become explicit when claims, workflows, and consequences grow
too complex to carry in individual memory. Popper's falsifiability criterion
supplies one sharp starting point. A claim should expose what observation could
count against it, not only what evidence would make it look stronger.[^fn-popper-falsifiability]

[^fn-popper-falsifiability]: Popper developed the criterion in *Logik der
Forschung*, published in German in 1934 and later in English as *The Logic of
Scientific Discovery* [@Popper2002LogicScientificDiscovery]. Here
falsifiability belongs to an architectural claim and its test protocol, not to
an AI model as an intrinsic property. It asks what observable result would
conflict with the claim. It does not imply that one failed run establishes the
root cause or ends the inquiry.

Not every architecture decision is a universal scientific hypothesis. The
transferable requirement is more modest and more useful. State the scope, name
the observation that would count against the claim, and keep the decision open
to revision when stronger evidence arrives.

| **External practice** | **What it makes explicit** | **What transfers to Architecture 2.0** | **Where the analogy stops** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Experimental science | A claim, test conditions, observations that could conflict with it, and provisional rather than final acceptance [@Popper2002LogicScientificDiscovery]. | State the claim and non-claim, predeclare counterevidence, and preserve results that weaken the preferred explanation. | Tool failure, model mismatch, and scope error can also explain a failed run, so rejection still requires diagnosis. |
| Systems safety and assurance | System constraints, process assumptions, control and feedback paths, evidence arguments, responsibility, and waivers [@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld; @Kelly2004GSN]. | Review the whole sociotechnical loop rather than treating a capable model or passing component check as sufficient. | Most architecture exploration is not safety certification; evidence and independence should scale with consequence. |
| High-energy physics | Data, code, computational environment, workflow steps, provenance, versions, and internal review state captured during the analysis [@ChenEtAl2016CERNAnalysisPreservation]. | Preserve versioned design trajectories and runnable inputs while the work is happening, not as a reconstruction after publication. | A preserved workflow can support replay without establishing that its claim, baseline, or decision was adequate. |
| Translational bioinformatics failure analysis | Raw data, transformations, labels, and code needed to reconstruct a predictor after undocumented processing hid simple errors; in the cited case, trial allocation was already occurring [@BaggerlyCoombes2009]. | Make transformations inspectable and establish credible challenge before a claim justifies consequential commitment. | The cited case exposed failure after clinical use began; it does not show that pre-use independent reanalysis was already standard practice. |
| Site-reliability engineering | Observability, error budgets, runbooks, rollback, and revalidation under drift [@BeyerEtAl2016SRE]. | Expand automated authority where actions are monitored and reversible, and narrow it as rollback becomes costly. | Silicon, interfaces, and long-lived hardware commitments are not as cheaply reversible as software deployments. |
| Scientific databases and benchmarks | Shared data, metadata, interfaces, workloads, and reporting rules that let results travel across groups [@JainEtAl2013MaterialsProject; @SPEC2017CPU; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. | Build common datasets and comparison protocols around explicit task and evidence scope. | A dataset or leaderboard alone does not record why a design was rejected or who authorized the resulting claim. |

: **Other fields converge on inspectable claims, workflows, evidence, challenge, and decisions:** Architecture 2.0 borrows these properties without importing another field's institutions or vocabulary unchanged. {#tbl-cross-field-method-lineage tbl-colwidths="[18,27,29,18]"}

@tbl-cross-field-method-lineage is a lineage map, not another checklist. It
also answers why a structured architecture study is more than retained logs.
Logs can preserve observations and provenance, but they do not by themselves
state the question and comparison, expose missing alternatives, name
counterevidence, explain the architectural mechanism, establish a credible
challenge path, or authorize a decision. Those relationships are part of the
engineering method. Architecture 2.0 makes them reviewable together rather
than treating a pile of traces as reasoning.

This chapter gives the reusable language for that shift. The goal is not to
classify every paper or tool. A taxonomy of current systems will age quickly.
The more durable contribution is a consistent way to bound an architecture
study and state its claim, together with an ontology of the entities and
relationships that make AI participation inspectable.

The ontology has to earn its space by being useful. A researcher should be
able to cite it when explaining the structure of an Architecture 2.0
contribution. A reviewer should be able to use it to reconstruct the study,
inspect the claim, and locate its evidence and decision boundary. A tool
builder should be able to use it to specify a harness or environment. An
author should be able to use it to state what AI contributed to a concrete
design problem. If the ontology cannot support those uses, it is only
vocabulary. Its test is whether it exposes enough state for a method to act
within bounds and enough evidence for an accountable reviewer to challenge the
claim and judge the proposed decision.

The deeper reason we need such an ontology is that architecture lacks a widely
adopted cross-tool layer that connects formulation, experiments, evidence, and
decisions above RTL. Below RTL, design flows already have artifacts that tools
can parse, transform, reject, and sign off. Although high-level synthesis (HLS)
flows, modeling languages, compiler IRs, and EDA frameworks structure parts of
the path to implementation, much of the reasoning that decides *what* should be
built still lives in whiteboards, spreadsheets, scripts, slides, review
memories, and natural-language specifications. The design-loop card gives a
reviewer one compact index into that work. It can point to the study's tool
contract (@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces) and detailed
evidence record without copying either into another form.

Compiler infrastructure gives a useful but limited analogy. MLIR, a multi-level intermediate representation framework for building compilers, made many compiler transformations inspectable
[@LattnerEtAl2020MLIR]. Architecture 2.0 is not asking for one universal
architecture IR. It is asking for a reviewable claim-and-evidence layer where
design intent, workload scope, feedback, and challenge can be checked before an
artifact is trusted.

The hierarchy in this chapter is simple. A bounded architecture study is the
unit of work. Its intended outcome is better architectural understanding,
artifacts, findings, or decisions. An artifact is an implementation or proposal
the study produces, and the architectural claim is the unit another architect
reviews. A study may use a fixed execution and analysis mechanism; when the
work iterates, that mechanism is a design loop. The ontology names the important entities and relationships. The
design-loop card is a compact index into the study, claim, artifacts, evidence,
and decision. It is an instrument, not the intellectual result.

That layer sits between tacit reasoning and implementation flows
(@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl). Its purpose is not to add ceremony. It is to make the
part of architecture reasoning above RTL explicit enough that tools, automated participants,
reviewers, and architects can all see the same study state.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can make a bounded AI-assisted architecture study
reviewable. That means you can:

- **Bound:** State the study's workload, baseline, design space, constraints, non-goals, and intended decision.
- **Challenge:** State an architectural claim together with supporting evidence and observations that would weaken or overturn it.
- **Distinguish:** Separate the study, its artifacts, its claim, its execution loop, and its supporting records.
- **Map:** Connect bounded AI jobs to represented state, legal actions, tools, feedback, evidence, and accountable authority.
- **Use:** Read the ontology and design-loop card diagnostically without treating completion of either as proof of architectural quality.
:::

![**A reviewable architecture study connects intent to implementation evidence:** The bounded study contains the scope, architectural claim, execution and analysis mechanism, and evidence needed for review. Iterative work uses a design loop. A compact card indexes these records without replacing them.](images/F4d-structured-layer-above-rtl){#fig-structured-layer-above-rtl width="100%" fig-alt="Three-layer diagram showing tacit architecture reasoning flowing into a reviewable architecture study, which connects to executable tools and returns implementation evidence for review."}

## The Bounded Study Is the Unit of Work

> **Bounded architecture study.** A bounded architecture study is a scoped unit
> of architecture work that names its question, workload or scenario, baseline,
> legal design space, objectives, constraints, non-goals, representations and
> tools, planned evidence, and intended decision.

A study may produce better architectural understanding, an implementation, a
design-space map, a finding, or a decision. Those outcomes need not all take the
same form, but another architect should be able to recover the comparison that
made them meaningful and the evidence that limits what may be concluded. The
study is therefore broader than the artifact it produces and broader than the
design loop used to carry out the work.

## The Architectural Claim Is the Unit of Review

Asking whether a generative model[^fn-ml-generative-model] can design hardware is too coarse a framing. The more
architecture-native approach focuses on identifying the specific claim being made and determining the evidence required to make that claim credible. Architects rarely accept an artifact by itself. They
accept or reject a claim about that artifact relative to a workload, baseline,
design space, objectives, constraints, and evidence. The definition below names
that review object so the rest of the chapter can ask what an AI-assisted result
actually establishes.

[^fn-ml-generative-model]: A class of AI models designed to generate new data, such as text or configurations, that resembles its training data. In an architecture design loop such a model proposes candidate artifacts inside the legal design space, which is why the chapter insists its output still be tested as a claim rather than trusted as a result.

> **Architectural claim.** An architectural claim states that an artifact,
> mechanism, relationship, finding, or decision improves, preserves, explains,
> or rules out specified hardware/software behavior for a workload or scenario
> relative to a baseline. It carries the legal design space, objectives,
> constraints, claim boundary, supporting evidence, and observations that would
> weaken or overturn it.

> **Rejection authority, conditions, and checks.** A rejection condition names
> an observable result
> that counts against a candidate or claim. A rejection check is the test, tool,
> rule, or review that evaluates that condition. Rejection authority is the
> declared power to block, weaken, or redirect advancement, which may be
> automated under an explicit policy. The accountable decision owner remains
> responsible for consequential commitment, exceptions, and waivers. The
> strongest supported claim and the authority to act on it remain separate.

> **Commitment boundary.** The commitment boundary is the strongest next
> decision the current evidence permits.

The clause "for a specified workload or scenario" is doing real work, and a neighboring field paid to learn it.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Valid for the last rocket, not this one"}
The Ariane 5 rocket reused inertial-reference software that had flown on Ariane 4. Those flights supplied operational evidence inside the Ariane 4 envelope, not a correctness guarantee for a new vehicle. On Ariane 5's different, higher-velocity trajectory, a value overflowed a conversion the older profile never reached, both channels shut down, and the vehicle self-destructed about forty seconds after launch. The inquiry traced the loss to a software specification and design error together with tests that did not represent Ariane 5 flight conditions [@Lions1996Ariane5]. The prior evidence did not cover the scenario to which the component was now committed.

**Lesson:** A claim carries the scenario it was verified under, so an AI-assisted study that reuses a cheap, trusted component still has to re-check its evidence against the new workload, because the claim, not the code, is what has to hold.
:::

A plain-language version might say that this candidate improves useful work for
this workload, relative to this baseline, inside this design space, under these
constraints, using this evidence, and subject to these rejection checks and
decision rights. The following tuple is optional notation for that sentence:
$$
\mathcal{C} =
\langle P, W, B, \mathcal{D}, \mathbf{J}, \mathcal{K}, E, R, M, H \rangle .
$$
Here, $P$ is the proposition under review, $W$ is the workload or scenario,
$B$ is the baseline, $\mathcal{D}$
is the legal design space, $\mathbf{J}$ is the objective vector, $\mathcal{K}$
is the constraint set, $E$ is the evidence, $R$ is the declared rejection
conditions, checks, and authority, $M$ is the commitment boundary, and $H$ is
the accountable decision authority. The notation is a claim-focused
explanation, not a second schema or a separate checklist. Its purpose is to prevent a generated
artifact from masquerading as an architectural result before the comparison,
constraints, evidence, rejection checks, decision rights, and commitment
boundary are visible.

@tbl-architectural-claim-schema applies that same view to the lighthouse prompt.
It explains what a reviewer needs the study record to state or point to, not a
set of additional machine-required fields or another form for the reader.

| **Claim field** | **Reader question** | **Lighthouse pre-execution protocol** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Claim proposition** | What exact relationship, finding, or decision is asserted, and what is not claimed? | Within the bounded three-organization comparison, a shared-memory SoC block can improve mobile XR efficiency relative to a vector CPU extension and a loosely coupled accelerator while remaining within 3\ W and preserving QoS and deployability. This does not claim full-SoC or tapeout readiness. |
| **Workload or scenario** | What behavior is the design supposed to serve? | XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workloads, with latency, sensing, graphics, and interaction requirements. |
| **Baseline** | Compared to what architecture, software stack, or prior result? | The vector CPU extension is the reference point inside the lighthouse sequence's bounded comparison with a loosely coupled accelerator and a shared-memory SoC block. All three use the same workload slice, software assumptions, and power envelope. |
| **Design space** | What choices are legal, invalid, deferred, or out of scope? | Open-standard RISC-V ISA options, vector width, CPU/accelerator partitioning, memory hierarchy, clocking, compiler/runtime path, and tool-flow limits. |
| **Objective vector** | What counts as improvement, and what tradeoffs matter? | Throughput, tail latency, energy, area, programmability, verification burden, and evidence cost under the 3\ W target. |
| **Constraints** | What cannot be violated even if a metric improves? | ISA compatibility, correctness, thermal limits, process assumptions, package limits, software compatibility, and 3\ nm-class low-power envelope. |
| **Evidence** | What supports the claim at the required commitment level? | Planned records include workload traces, simulations, a power model, sensitivity checks, rejected candidates, tool logs, and three-way comparisons. No empirical result is claimed at this point. |
| **Rejection checks and authority** | What result counts against the claim, which check tests it, and what may block or redirect advancement? | A latency or power violation, invalid RTL or configuration, compiler failure, simulator mismatch, or weak coverage, applied through declared tests, tools, and review policy. |
| **Commitment boundary** | What is the strongest next decision the evidence permits, and what remains uncommitted? | Advance at most to a higher-fidelity modeling or RTL-study question. Implementation, deployment, and silicon-facing commitments remain outside this study. |
| **Accountable decision authority** | Who can accept, revise, escalate, waive, or commit the claim? | A named architect, project review lead, or course instructor owns assumptions, evidence thresholds, exceptions, and the decision to advance the study. |

: **A pre-execution claim protocol makes a proposed study reviewable:** This claim-focused view shows the planned comparison, scope, evidence, rejection checks, decision rights, and exploration-only commitment boundary before any result exists. {#tbl-architectural-claim-schema tbl-colwidths="[18,33,39]"}

::: {.callout-war-story title="A claim with missing review fields"}
**The reported result.** The 2021 paper behind AlphaChip, Google's reinforcement-learning system for chip floorplanning, states a clear macro-placement task and reports placements at expert quality [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The disclosure gap.** Cheng and colleagues report that the original data and code were not fully available and that key black-box elements had to be reconstructed. Their assessment supplied open test cases and evaluation flows, but it could not turn the original public record into a like-for-like comparison under one common flow [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL].

**The lesson.** Those gaps do not by themselves show that the reported result is wrong. They bound what an independent reviewer can establish from the public record. A stronger public claim needs a shared baseline, inspectable tool and data bindings, higher-fidelity evidence, and a common evaluation protocol. State the claim as a review object and the dispute becomes specific about the missing evidence.
:::

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

This schema also clarifies what AI systems are being asked to do. Generation
can propose artifacts inside $\mathcal{D}$. Prediction can estimate
components of $\mathbf{J}$ before expensive feedback. Optimization can search
tradeoffs under $\mathcal{K}$. Critique and verification can apply $R$.
The architectural result may be understanding, an artifact, a finding, or a
decision. The claim binds that result to its comparison, scope, and evidence so
another architect can review what it establishes.

::: {#pri-state-the-claim .callout-design-principle title="State the claim as a review object"}
An AI-assisted architectural result is credible only when the bounded study
names its proposition, workload, baseline, design space, objective, constraints,
evidence, rejection checks, and decision owner. Until it states those, the AI
output may still be useful, but no reviewable architectural claim about the
artifact has been established.
:::

## The Design Loop Is an Execution and Analysis Mechanism

Once the claim is explicit, the architect must define the study's execution and
analysis mechanism. When the work iterates across AI methods and tools, that
mechanism is a design loop. The distinction matters because such work is not a
single act of generation. It frames a problem, chooses abstractions, explores
alternatives, measures candidates, rejects weak results, revises assumptions,
and decides when evidence is strong enough to commit.

> **Architecture design loop.** An architecture design loop is a repeated
> process within a bounded study that carries represented architecture state
> through permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, review, and revision. It
> helps produce understanding, artifacts, findings, or decisions while
> preserving the grounds for another turn.

For Architecture 2.0, this is one mechanism through which an AI system
participates. If its state, actions, feedback, and stopping rules are implicit,
the system is only producing outputs, not participating credibly in the study.

For the lighthouse prompt, the distinction is immediate. A request for a
low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for XRBench-class mobile XR, a mixed-reality workload suite,
under a 3\ W, 3\ nm-class low-power mobile envelope sounds compact. But the
prompt does not yet define a bounded study. It does not say which workload traces
are authoritative, which vector operations matter, which memory hierarchy is
admissible, which software stack must run, which simulator is trusted, which
power model applies, which process assumptions are available, which
alternatives must be considered, or what evidence is enough to reject a
candidate. These are not details to add after a generative method responds. They are the
architecture problem.

One turn connects represented state to a bounded action, an observation from the
environment, a record that may support an evidence judgment, and a decision to
accept, reject, revise, or escalate. Objectives and constraints bound the turn; stopping and
escalation rules determine whether another turn is justified. The following
equation is only a compact explanation of that update:
$$
s_{t+1} =
\operatorname{Update}(s_t, a_t, o_t, e_t, d_t).
$$
Here, $s_t$ is the represented architecture state, $a_t$ is the bounded
action taken by the loop, $o_t$ is the observation returned by the
environment, and $e_t$ is an evidence entry only when the observation is bound
to the claim, scope, interpretation, and provenance needed for review. The term
$d_t$ is the human or policy decision to accept, reject,
revise, or escalate. This equation is not a schema and does not claim that every architecture
loop is a Markov decision process[^fn-rl-mdp]. It is a bookkeeping view of the
same turn summarized by the design-loop card. If a loop
cannot say how actions, feedback, evidence, and decisions update state, then
it is not yet represented well enough for credible AI-mediated architecture
work (@fig-design-loop).

[^fn-rl-mdp]: A mathematical framework for modeling decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision maker; it forms the foundation of reinforcement learning. The chapter borrows the loop's notation from this framework while cautioning that an architecture loop need not literally be one.

![**A design loop organizes execution and analysis when work iterates:** Represented state, bounded action, tool feedback, and evidence support review of an architectural claim. Review may advance or narrow the claim, reject it, or revise the study. Fidelity, commitment, and allowed authority remain separate choices.](images/F3-design-loop){#fig-design-loop width="100%" fig-alt="A design loop within a bounded architecture study. Represented state, bounded action, tool feedback, and evidence lead to claim review. A bounded finding or decision can leave the loop, while revision updates the study state for another turn."}

The bounded study remains the unit of work, and its architectural claim remains
the unit of review. Process analysis examines the design loop because a model
is only one participant in it. A method may generate candidates, summarize
evidence, predict outcomes, call tools, critique assumptions, or coordinate
subtasks. Its output becomes credible through the study's comparisons,
experiments, and review, not through the model in isolation.

Cross-layer reach makes that distinction concrete. An architecture decision
moves through the whole stack at once. A choice that is legal at the
microarchitecture level can still require a compiler change above it or create
a timing and routing bottleneck below it. No participant, human or automated,
should be presumed to possess complete state across those layers. The study must
therefore expose the relevant assumptions and cross-layer consequences so a
candidate that looks valid in one layer can be challenged when it breaks
another.

## Design Spaces Make Claims Meaningful

An architectural claim is meaningful only relative to a design space. A system
that reports "the best" candidate without exposing the alternatives, invalid
regions, baseline, and tradeoffs has not made an architecture result easy to
review. It has hidden the comparison that gives the result meaning.

In architecture, the design space is not the set of all strings a model might
emit. It is a constrained set of architecturally legal choices:
$$
\mathcal{D} = \{x \in X \mid x \text{ is valid under the task and architecture
constraints}\}.
$$
Here, $X$ is that unconstrained space of candidate artifacts, everything a
method could emit. The validity conditions may include ISA compatibility, memory semantics,
software support, timing assumptions, power limits, package constraints,
verification requirements, and deployment policy. The current representation
and toolchain usually support only an executable subset of $\mathcal{D}$. A
candidate inside the legal space but outside that subset exposes an environment
or representation gap; it should be deferred or evaluated through another path,
not mislabeled as architecturally invalid. A candidate outside $\mathcal{D}$ is
invalid for the stated study unless the study explicitly revises its scope or
constraints and records why.

The lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. A 64-bit RISC-V-based mobile XR
subsystem might vary vector width, cache and memory hierarchy, accelerator
partitioning, dataflow, clocking, voltage assumptions, compiler/runtime
support, and verification scope. Some choices are legal but unattractive. Some
are attractive under a proxy but fail at higher fidelity. Some violate the
power envelope, process assumptions, software contract, or workload coverage.
An Architecture 2.0 study must represent those distinctions. Otherwise its
methods cannot distinguish improvement from exploitation of a hole in the
problem statement.

The design space is also where multiobjective efficiency enters the ontology. The objective
is rarely a scalar reward. It is a vector of performance, energy, latency,
area, reliability, programmability, verification burden, cost, and evidence
requirements. A design-space report is therefore a study artifact that points
to evidence. It should show what was explored, what was rejected, what tradeoffs
remain, and what the architect must still decide.

## The Architecture 2.0 Ontology {#sec-arch2-ontology}

A method label is not enough to review an architecture claim. A paper can say
it uses an LLM, reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimization], or a surrogate
model and still leave the important relationships invisible. The reader does
not yet know what task was bounded, what actions were legal, what feedback was
used, what failed, or who accepted the commitment. A taxonomy can group methods,
benchmarks, tools, or tasks, but those categories will change with the field.

[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimization]: A strategy for optimizing expensive black-box functions, where each candidate evaluation (such as a cycle-accurate simulation) is costly. The chapter names it as one method a paper might cite, and stresses that the label matters less than the represented study state, available evaluation resources, and challenge rules the method operates under.

> **Architecture 2.0 ontology.** The Architecture 2.0 ontology is a conceptual
> relationship map. It names the entities and relationships that make a bounded
> AI-assisted architecture study representable, executable, reviewable, and
> accountable; it is not a formal machine-interpretable ontology.

As a preview of @sec-data-representations-world-models, a world model is an
architecture-scoped belief about how actions change outcomes
[@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels; @SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. It may
live in a simulator, surrogate, cost model, or design rules.
@sec-data-representations-world-models gives the canonical definition and
treatment.

The important content is relational. Intent constrains tasks.
Tasks determine what must be represented. Representation limits what study
participants can observe and modify. That scoped belief encodes how actions
change outcomes. Tools and environments define valid actions and measurable
feedback. Methods are selected for the task, representation, and feedback
limits. Feedback becomes evidence only when fidelity, provenance, uncertainty,
and relevance are understood. Declared decision processes accept, reject,
revise, or escalate the result. The ontology names those relationships. The
design-loop card indexes them and points to supporting records; there is no
separate ontology form to complete.

The supporting evidence record captures what the study actually observed and how that
observation affected the claim. @sec-feedback-verification-trust owns the full
treatment of feedback and evidence.

This is why ontology should precede taxonomy. Before classifying whether a paper
uses an LLM[^fn-ml-llm], reinforcement learning[^fn-rl-method-label], Bayesian
optimization, a surrogate, or a simulator wrapper, identify the bounded study
and its claim. Once the task, actions, feedback, evidence, challenge, and decision relationships
are visible, method categories become useful. Before that, labels can hide more
than they reveal.

[^fn-ml-llm]: Large Language Model, a neural network trained on vast amounts of text to understand and generate human-like language. Here it names one method label a paper might advertise, which the chapter argues reveals little until the study, role, and represented state are identified.

[^fn-rl-method-label]: For Architecture 2.0, the label "reinforcement learning" still does not say what state the agent saw, which actions were legal, which reward proxied the objective, or which rejection conditions bounded the loop.

The practical implication is not to build a chip-specific language model[^fn-ml-language-model] first.
A field becomes AI-addressable when its objects of work, legal actions,
feedback, evidence, challenge rules, and commitment boundaries are represented
well enough for methods to act and experts to judge. That is why this book
starts with the structure of architecture work rather than a catalog of models.

[^fn-ml-language-model]: A statistical model trained to predict the next word or token in a sequence, forming the foundation of modern AI text generation. The chapter's point is that building such a domain-specific model is not the precondition for AI-addressable architecture; representing the loop's objects, actions, and evidence is.

## The Compact Framework

@fig-ontology-chain shows the chapter's argument in one view. The study bounds
the question and alternatives. The architectural claim defines what must be
reviewed. Representation, tools, and method assignments determine what can be
done, and evidence plus accountable review determine what may be concluded.

![**The framework connects a bounded study to an architectural decision:** The study establishes scope, the claim defines what must be reviewed, and representations and environments make action possible. When work iterates, a design loop organizes execution and analysis. Evidence supports accountable review, which may advance a finding, narrow the claim, or revise the study.](images/F4-ontology-chain){#fig-ontology-chain width="100%" fig-alt="A framework connecting a bounded architecture study and its architectural claim to representations, tools, an execution and analysis mechanism, evidence, accountable review, and a bounded finding or decision."}

The architectural claim, not the model output, is the review object. For
practical work, the twelve-field design-loop card provides one compact index.
The claim tuple, state
equation, figures, and grouped questions in this chapter explain different
relationships inside that card; none is another schema.

The framework can be read in three passes. The first establishes scope through
intent, task, and design space. The second exposes execution through
representation, environment, method assignment, and evaluation limits. The
third judges the result through evidence, failed or rejected alternatives,
rejection checks and authority, commitment boundary, and decision. Scope supports problem
formulation. Execution examines exploration, implementation, and evaluation.
Judgment examines explanation and defense as well as the decision itself. These
are review lenses, not sequential stages.
@tbl-compact-framework-checklist applies them to the lighthouse prompt.

| **Reading pass** | **Reader question** | **Lighthouse instance** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Scope** | What claim is being made, for which workload and baseline, inside which legal design space? | Improve mobile XR efficiency within a 3\ W, 3\ nm-class low-power mobile envelope while keeping the workload, baseline, legal RISC-V choices, and deferred work explicit. |
| **Execution** | What state can the loop read and change, what job does each method perform, and what feedback can it afford? | Workload traces, architecture parameters, compiler assumptions, a simulator or cost model, bounded candidate generation or search, and a declared evaluation budget. |
| **Judgment** | What supports or challenges the claim, what mechanism explains the result, and what commitment follows? | Pareto and sensitivity evidence, rejected candidates, an explanation grounded in utilization and memory traffic, declared rejection checks, and a bounded architectural decision. |

: **The framework can be read in three passes:** Scope, execution, and judgment group the canonical card fields for review without creating another schema. {#tbl-compact-framework-checklist tbl-colwidths="[20,36,34]"}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

These reading passes are intentionally stricter than many current
demonstrations. A system can be useful while still leaving its action bounds,
evidence standard, rejection checks, decision rights, or commitment
underspecified.

The practical index is the design-loop card developed below and expanded in
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card. The card is not a new concept
on top of the framework. @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card also gives the card a
machine-checkable schema and four structural conformance levels, from
context-only to records with declared independence and decision fields. A validator can confirm
that required fields and links are present. It cannot establish architectural
quality, novelty, insight, correctness, or adequate judgment.

The translation aid in @tbl-architecture-translation-matrix keeps the vocabulary from drifting into generic AI
language. Words such as state, action, observation, environment, reward, and
critic are useful only after they are translated into architecture objects
and checks. It is not another review form.
If a paper says an automated method acts in an environment, the reader should be able to
name the architecture state it reads, the action it is allowed to take, the
tool feedback it observes, the result that would count against the claim, and
the declared check and decision process.

| **Generic term** | **Architecture translation** | **Example artifacts or observations** | **Failure or challenge condition** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **State** | Workload, design, software, tool, constraint, and evidence state. | Traces, configs, RTL, compiler IR, simulator stats, EDA reports, review notes. | Missing provenance or hidden assumptions. |
| **Action** | Legal architecture, compiler, runtime, or tool-flow change. | Change cache size, vector width, mapping, schedule, constraint, partition, or test. | Invalid parameter, noncompilable code, nonsynthesizable RTL, or policy violation. |
| **Observation** | Feedback returned by a tool, benchmark, review, or deployment path. | Latency, energy, area, timing, congestion, warnings, failures, telemetry. | Wrong workload, stale tool version, simulator mismatch, or weak fidelity. |
| **Environment** | Tool-connected harness that defines legal actions and feedback. | Simulator wrapper, compiler pipeline, RTL flow, EDA stage, benchmark harness. | Unmodeled constraints, nondeterminism, incomplete logging, or invalid actions. |
| **Objective** | Explicit architecture tradeoff, not a generic reward. | Performance, power, and area; tail latency; power envelope; reliability; carbon; cost; evidence budget. | Proxy gaming, lost Pareto tradeoff, or missing accountable decision rule. |
| **Critic/verifier** | Declared challenge check that can weaken or reject a claim; independence is required when the consequence warrants it. | Tests, formal checks, baseline replay, cross-simulator comparison, signoff review. | Unsupported claim, failed check, counterexample, or insufficient evidence. |

: **AI terms need architecture translations:** Architecture 2.0 uses generic vocabulary only when each term is grounded in concrete hardware/software design objects, tool outputs, and challenge mechanisms. {#tbl-architecture-translation-matrix tbl-colwidths="[16,28,28,18]"}

These relationships do not form a pipeline that runs once. A failed
simulation may revise the representation. A weak benchmark result may revise
the task. A provenance problem may invalidate the evidence. A rejection may
change the environment, not merely reject a candidate. Architecture 2.0 is
therefore not only about adding AI into existing work. It makes that
participation bounded, observable, and accountable within an architecture
study.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Study Revision Check"}
When an AI method fails or produces an invalid result, the architect must decide whether the failure rejects only the candidate or requires revising the environment, design space, or evidence standard.
:::

## Authority Is Scoped, Not Inherent

Autonomous or non-autonomous is too coarse a description. Authority is granted
to a bounded assignment within a study, not possessed by a model as a
personality trait. @fig-autonomy-ladder-human-agent shows four representative
scopes, from assisted work to bounded phase execution. They are neither a
schema nor a maturity score. Broader authority is credible only when the study
exposes allowed actions, observable feedback, evidence requirements, rollback
or escalation, decision rights, and the commitment boundary. The same test
applies whether one automated participant acts or several coordinate.

![**AI authority is scoped to a bounded study:** These scopes describe permission for particular actions and decisions, not a maturity ladder or a property of the model. Broader authority requires explicit bounds, observable feedback, sufficient evidence, escalation or rollback, and declared decision rights.](images/F4c-autonomy-ladder-human-agent){#fig-autonomy-ladder-human-agent width="100%" fig-alt="Four scopes of AI authority within a bounded architecture study, from assisted work to bounded phase execution, with explicit action bounds, feedback, evidence, escalation or rollback, and decision rights."}

The appropriate stage depends on architecture-specific risk. A compiler flag
that can be rolled back after telemetry is not the same as an RTL change that
affects timing closure. A simulator configuration is not the same as a
mask-level choice. A benchmark-generation loop is not the same as a signoff
loop. The more irreversible the action, the stronger the evidence, independent
challenge, and accountable decision process must be.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint}
Before granting an AI method broader authority, locate the allowed actions,
evidence requirement, rejection or escalation path, accountable owner, and
commitment it may not cross. If any is missing, the authority is not yet
justified.
:::

The remaining sections preview conditions developed in
@sec-data-representations-world-models,
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces,
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization, and
@sec-feedback-verification-trust.

## Intent Defines the Task

Architecture tasks do not appear naturally. They are carved out of messy
intent. A product goal such as "improve mobile XR efficiency" is not yet a task.
It must become bounded work, such as characterizing a workload, comparing
compute organizations, estimating memory traffic, or preparing a design-space
report.

This translation is architectural judgment. It decides what is in scope, what
is out of scope, what can be measured, and what cost of being wrong is
acceptable. It also sets how much authority an AI method may exercise. Critiquing a report,
predicting energy, searching an accelerator tiling space, and editing RTL need
different state, feedback, and rejection conditions. "Use AI" is therefore
never the task. The study must bound the work before a method is chosen; a card
can index that boundary for review.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization later develops the bounded jobs
that methods can perform.

## Representations and World Models

Representation is the first hard problem because it determines what study
participants can see. Architecture knowledge lives in many forms, including natural-language
specifications, ISA documents, traces, graphs, simulator configurations, RTL,
compiler IR, EDA reports, benchmark metadata, scripts, and review notes. Hidden
defaults, undocumented constraints, and forgotten rejections create state an
automated participant cannot inspect. The result may be invalid, misleading, or
impossible to replay even when the generated artifact looks plausible.

This preview follows the distinction defined in @sec-data-representations-world-models, where a world model is different from a representation. The representation says what
is encoded; the previewed world model says what the loop believes will happen after an
action. A simulator, learned surrogate, calibrated equation, or design rule may
play that role, and each has a limited scope and failure mode.

QuArch, an architecture-centric benchmark dataset, makes the boundary concrete.
It can assess whether a model knows architecture concepts, but not whether an
automated participant has the represented workload, tool, action, evidence, and challenge state
needed to synthesize or reject a candidate
[@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch]. That distinction is the bridge to
@sec-data-representations-world-models.

## Tools Become Environments

Architecture tools become Architecture 2.0 environments when they define how an
automated participant or method can act. A simulator, compiler, profiler, RTL tool, EDA flow,
runtime system, or fleet telemetry pipeline does more than return a number. It
defines which actions are legal, how long feedback takes, what observations are
available, what costs are incurred, what provenance is recorded, and what
failure means.

The distinction becomes a visual test (@fig-tools-become-environments-preview). A wrapper connects a method to a tool, while an environment exposes the
contract that makes action, feedback, failure, and provenance inspectable.

![**An architecture environment exposes the contract needed for bounded action:** Legal actions, observations, costs, fidelity, provenance, invalid-action behavior, and failure signals make tool feedback inspectable. The contract does not itself turn feedback into evidence or decide the architectural claim.](images/F4e-tools-become-environments-preview){#fig-tools-become-environments-preview width="100%" fig-alt="Side-by-side diagram contrasting a basic tool wrapper with an architecture environment that exposes legal actions, observations, cost and fidelity, provenance, invalid-action behavior, and failure signals."}

ArchGym, an open-source evaluation environment, is an important example because
it treats the connection between search algorithms and architecture simulators as
a first-class interface [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Its durable lesson is that a
simulator wrapper becomes architectural only when it exposes legal actions,
invalid-action semantics, feedback cost, provenance, comparable baselines, and
failure signals. @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces expands
this point and asks what such environments can and cannot prove.

## Agents and Methods Have Roles in a Compound System

The word "agent" can hide too much. In credible Architecture 2.0 systems, there
may be several roles rather than one monolithic actor. A generator proposes
candidates. A predictor estimates behavior before expensive evaluation. An
optimizer chooses what to try next. A critic challenges assumptions. A verifier
checks constraints. A planner decomposes work. A tool caller executes actions.
A coordinator tracks state, provenance, and dependencies. An architect or
accountable engineering team sets intent, declares decision rights, and decides
what evidence is enough for consequential claims.

Language models, search procedures, learned surrogates, scripts, formal tools,
and people can fill those roles in different combinations. The review question
is not which mechanism is fashionable. It is what role acts, what state it
consumes, what action it takes, and what checks or challenges its output.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization develops these compositions.

The architect's role does not depend on a prediction that AI will remain unable
to perform these jobs. An architecture study joins empirical results to choices
about objectives, adequate abstractions, cross-layer tradeoffs, acceptable risk,
and organizational commitment. AI may perform most of the execution and may
propose those judgments. The choices still have to be represented, challenged,
and owned by an accountable person or engineering organization. This does not require a
person to approve every action. It requires explicit responsibility for the
study's objectives, exceptions, and consequential decisions.

## Feedback Becomes Evidence

Feedback is not evidence by default. A simulator result, benchmark score,
synthesis report, generated explanation, or model confidence value is feedback.
It becomes evidence only when it is tied to a claim, its scope, an
interpretation, and a provenance trail.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="A 3 W claim needs evidence, not a number"}
**Context.** A reported power result is feedback. It becomes evidence only when
the study records enough state for a reviewer to judge what the number means.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** If a generative method claims that a "vector-capable CPU,
accelerator, or SoC block" for the "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload"
meets the "3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP mobile process," the study must
record the workload, input distribution, memory traffic, power model, process
assumptions, compiled software stack, rejected alternatives, uncertainty, and
the observation or check that would count against the claim.

**Evidence rule.** The same power number has different authority as a proxy
estimate, cycle-level simulation, synthesis result, post-layout estimate, or
silicon measurement. A proxy estimate may support exploration; post-layout or
silicon evidence is needed before stronger implementation or deployment
commitments.

**Takeaway.** The design-space report must say which evidence level supports the
claim and which commitment boundary it has not crossed.
:::

Evidence also includes negative information. Rejected candidates, failed
simulator runs, invalid configurations, proxy wins that disappear at higher
fidelity, and assumptions that had to be abandoned are not waste. They are
architecture data. They help explain why a surviving candidate deserves
attention and what would overturn the claim. @sec-feedback-verification-trust
develops the evidence, fidelity, and rejection rules this preview only names.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Feedback and Evidence Check"}
Before committing a model-generated candidate, locate the evidence pointer, the
result and check that could invalidate the candidate, the authority that may
stop advancement, and the accountable decision owner. If these are undefined,
the output cannot pass the commitment boundary.
:::

## The Design-Loop Card

> **Design-loop card.** A design-loop card is a compact index into one bounded
> architecture study. It states or points to the study's scope, architectural
> claim, execution mechanism, supporting and contrary evidence, rejection path,
> commitment boundary, and accountable next decision. Detailed state, evidence,
> and replay artifacts remain in linked records.

The card is one practical instrument for applying the ontology. It gives a
compact way to inspect a paper, project, benchmark, or internal study without
turning the full evidence record into another form. A reader may fill it in or
use it diagnostically. The claim tuple, state equation, ontology chain, and
grouped reading passes explain relationships; none is another schema.

A compact example for the lighthouse prompt appears in @fig-design-loop-card-example. The point is not that the card completes the design or contains the study. It gives a reviewer one place to find the claim, the study boundary, the execution path, and the records that support a decision.

![**A design-loop card indexes one bounded architecture study:** It points readers to the study scope, claim, execution mechanism, evidence, and accountable decision. Detailed state, evidence, and replay artifacts remain in linked records.](images/F4b-design-loop-card-example){#fig-design-loop-card-example width="100%" fig-alt="A compact study index for the lighthouse prompt, grouping the twelve canonical card fields under scope, execution mechanism, and claim review while pointing to detailed evidence and replay records."}

The card is deliberately simple. Its purpose is not to create paperwork. Its
purpose is to reveal whether a claimed Architecture 2.0 contribution exposes
enough of its study and claim for review. A paper that reports a better search result
but hides the cost and availability of feedback, rejected candidates, or
environment validity is hard to compare. A tool that produces designs but cannot say what rejects them
is hard to trust. A benchmark that measures model accuracy but not
architecture-relevant reasoning may be useful, but it should not be mistaken
for a complete architecture study.
Missing card fields signal missing disclosure, not automatic failure. Ask for
evidence, escalate fidelity, reject the claim, or narrow the commitment until
the study can support what it reports. A structurally complete card can still
index weak architecture. Completion is not evidence of quality, novelty,
insight, or correctness.

@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives the full 12-field card and review
rubric. Detailed evidence and runnable receipts remain linked artifacts rather
than additional card fields. The important point is that a major Architecture
2.0 claim should expose enough of its bounded study for another architect to
reconstruct and challenge it. The card is one compact index, not the review
object or the intellectual contribution.

## How the Rest of the Book Uses the Ontology

The remaining chapters unpack the ontology as missing conditions for credible
AI participation. @sec-data-representations-world-models asks what
architecture data must encode before AI systems can reason about it.
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces asks how simulators, compilers,
EDA flows, benchmarks, and deployment systems become action settings.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization asks which bounded method
assignments are valid under available evaluation resources.
@sec-feedback-verification-trust asks when
feedback becomes evidence strong enough for rejection and commitment.
@sec-running-the-loop constructs and analyzes a bounded lighthouse study through
one detailed design-loop turn; its synthetic evidence remains distinct from the
separate optional lab.
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack applies the framework across loop patterns in software, architecture
DSE, co-design, systems, and high-commitment silicon-facing work. @sec-what-architect-owns
returns to the architect, outlining what remains nondelegable, what the community must
build, and what it would mean for Architecture 2.0 to become a discipline
rather than a collection of demonstrations.

## Open Research Questions

The ontology turns the pressure from the previous chapter into researchable
questions. They ask what makes an AI-assisted architecture study inspectable,
which workload and baseline records make its claim credible, and what structured
review can catch before expensive execution.

1. **What minimum study record makes AI-assisted architecture work reviewable?**
An AI-assisted architecture study should expose its question, workload scope,
baseline, design space, represented state, environment, method assignments,
supporting and contrary evidence, challenge process, decision rights, and
commitment boundary. A strong contribution would test how much of that content
can be indexed without requiring the author's full internal toolchain. The
evaluation should measure whether independent reviewers identify missing
evidence, unsupported commitments, and unreviewable transitions more
consistently than they do from a normal paper artifact. The design-loop card in
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card provides one starting point.

2. **What workload and baseline records make a claim credible?**
Workload characterization becomes reviewable when a paper records input
distributions, benchmark construction, excluded cases, holdout workloads,
software versions, baseline assumptions, and provenance. A paper-quality result
would define a workload and baseline packet for AI-assisted architecture claims,
then test whether reviewers can tell where a result generalizes and where it
does not. This question sharpens the ontology by making workload scope part of
the claim rather than background context.

3. **How can holdout benchmarks expose study or method overfitting?**
An AI-assisted study can overfit its methods and conclusions to a benchmark
suite, trace slice, proxy model, or reviewer-visible metric. A strong research
artifact would define holdout construction methods and evaluate whether they
catch design-space lessons that fail on shifted workloads, changed baselines,
or stronger feedback. The result should tell reviewers when a method has learned
an architectural relationship and when it has learned the evaluation surface.

4. **How can rejected alternatives become auditable design-space evidence?**
Rejected candidates help bound the claim because they show which regions of
the design space were considered and why they failed. A research contribution
would define a record for rejected alternatives, invalid actions, failed
timing closures, proxy mismatches, and repaired candidates, then test whether
reviewers can reconstruct a design-space boundary from that record. This is the
negative-evidence version of the ontology in @sec-arch2-ontology.

5. **What can structured review catch before expensive execution?**
A study record should be useful before tools run, not only after a result
exists. A strong paper would evaluate which disclosed properties predict later
failure modes such as missing baselines, invalid action spaces, unsupported
evidence, or commitment boundaries that are too broad. The research artifact
could be a static checker, review protocol, authoring tool, or design-loop card
that flags under-specified claims while leaving architectural judgment with
accountable reviewers.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked what a bounded AI-assisted architecture study must expose
before its claims can be evaluated, challenged, and defended. The continuity
with classical practice lies in architecture's use of abstractions, tools,
experiments, measurement, and judgment. The shift is that AI can now
participate across formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation,
explanation, and defense, including parts of the process that produces the
claim.

The framework keeps five elements distinct. The bounded study is the unit of
work.
Understanding, artifacts, findings, and decisions are possible outcomes. The
architectural claim is the unit of review, binding an outcome to its workload,
baseline, design space, evidence, counterevidence, and decision boundary. The
design loop is the execution and analysis mechanism used when the study's work
iterates. The ontology, card, evidence record, checks, and runnable receipt are supporting
instruments that make the work inspectable at different depths.

Reviewability and replay remain separate. A runnable receipt binds executable
inputs, tool and environment versions, parameters, and outputs for an attempted
replay. Even a successful replay does not establish that the architectural
claim, baseline, or decision was adequate.

None of those instruments guarantees correctness or architectural value. A
complete record can support a weak claim, and a replayable experiment can answer
the wrong question. Their purpose is to expose what was represented, measured,
challenged, interpreted, and decided so another architect can locate the actual
grounds for the claim. That structure can outlast current models and tools
because it is anchored in architecture work rather than a particular AI method.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** A bounded architecture study organizes the work, an architectural claim organizes review, and a design loop organizes execution and analysis.
- **Reader test:** Can you identify the architecture question, AI role,
  alternatives, tool-connected experiments, claim, counterevidence,
  mechanism-based explanation, and bounded decision?
- **Up next:** @sec-data-representations-world-models asks what representations and world models must encode before AI methods can act on architecture state credibly.
:::
